display_name: GraphSpy: Hacker's Tooling Deep Dive (w/ creator @RedByte1337!)
author: John Hammond (feat. Keanu / RedByte1337)

parts: Device Code Phishing — Generating codes, tricking users via legitimate Microsoft login
Access Tokens & Refresh Tokens — JWT decoding, scopes, activation
OneDrive & SharePoint Enumeration — File browsing, credential harvesting
Outlook Graph Module — Full email read/send/reply via Graph API
Family of Client IDs (FOCI) — Token swapping between client IDs
MFA Method Manipulation — NextGen MFA claims, adding FIDO2 keys & authenticator apps
Microsoft Teams via Skype API — Reading/sending Teams messages
Entra ID Enumeration — Users, groups, dynamic groups, PIM role assignments
Device Registration & Primary Refresh Tokens (PRT) — SSO cookie generation
Windows Hello for Business — Persistent access without credentials
Social Engineering Tips — Vishing for device code phishing

key_takeaways: GraphSpy = web-based post-exploitation tool for Microsoft/Azure/Entra ID environments, works entirely with access tokens (no username/password needed)
Device Code Phishing: Generate device code → user authenticates on legitimate Microsoft login page → attacker gets access + refresh tokens instantly
Client ID selection matters: match it to your pretext (e.g., Microsoft Teams client ID for fake Teams invite)
Access tokens valid ~1 hour; Refresh tokens valid ~90 days — refresh tokens are more valuable
Family of Client IDs (FOCI): Microsoft deviates from OpenID Connect spec — refresh tokens from one client ID can generate access tokens for ANY client ID in the same family
GraphSpy provides full GUI for OneDrive browsing, SharePoint enumeration, Outlook email (read/send/reply), Teams messages — all via API calls with stolen tokens
Teams messages use legacy Skype API endpoint (not Graph API) because Graph API doesn't grant chat.read scope to Teams client ID
NextGen MFA claim: Special short-lived (~15 min) claim needed to modify MFA methods — obtainable via device code phishing with specific parameters
With NGC MFA claim: Can add FIDO2 security keys (YubiKey) or authenticator apps to victim's account — persistent access even after password reset
Identity-to-endpoint attack: Hijack OneDrive-synced shortcuts (e.g., replace Chrome shortcut with malicious PowerShell launcher)
Device Registration attack chain: Register new device → get device certificate → obtain Primary Refresh Token (PRT) → generate SSO cookies → sign into ANY Microsoft service without credentials
PRT cookies valid ~5 min but regeneratable indefinitely; resulting session cookies last hours to days
Windows Hello for Business key: Once registered, generates new PRTs indefinitely without username/password/MFA — ultimate persistence until device is removed from Azure portal
Dynamic groups in Entra ID: Membership rules based on user properties — some properties self-editable, enabling privilege escalation
Social engineering tip: Call target claiming to be helpdesk, direct them to microsoft.com/devicelogin (legitimate URL), talk them through entering your code — never ask for their password
